Indicates whether the contents of the given character sequences ocb @code s1 ccb and ocb @code s2 ccb are equal ignoring the case of any ASCII alphabetic characters between ocb @code 'a' ccb and ocb @code 'z' ccb or ocb @code 'A' ccb and ocb @code 'Z' ccb inclusive.

This method is significantly faster than ocb @link String#equalsIgnoreCase ccb and should be used in preference if at least one of the parameters is known to contain only ASCII characters.

Note however that this method does not always behave identically to expressions such as:

  • ocb @code string.toUpperCase op cp .equals op ""UPPER CASE ASCII"" cp ccb
  • ocb @code string.toLowerCase op cp .equals op ""lower case ascii"" cp ccb

due to case-folding of some non-ASCII characters op which does not occur in ocb @link String#equalsIgnoreCase ccb cp . However in almost all cases that ASCII strings are used the author probably wanted the behavior provided by this method rather than the subtle and sometimes surprising behavior of ocb @code toUpperCase op cp ccb and ocb @code toLowerCase op cp ccb .

Javadoc:
Close approximation of the frequently-used trigonometric method atan2. Average error is 1.057E-6 radians maximum error is 1.922E-6. Takes y and x op in that unusual order cp as floats and returns the angle from the origin to that point in radians. It is about 4 times faster than ocb @link Math#atan2 op double double cp ccb op roughly 15 ns instead of roughly 60 ns for Math on Java 8 HotSpot cp .
Credit for this goes to the 1955 research study ""Approximations for Digital Computers "" by RAND Corporation. This is sheet 11's algorithm which is the fourth-fastest and fourth-least precise. The algorithms on sheets 8-10 are faster but only by a very small degree and are considerably less precise. That study provides an ocb @link #atan op float cp ccb method and that cleanly translates to atan2 op cp .

Close approximation of the frequently-used trigonometric method atan2 using non-negative degrees only. Average absolute error is 0.00006045 degrees relative error is 0 degrees maximum error is 0.00011178 degrees. Takes y and x op in that unusual order cp as floats and returns the angle from the origin to that point in degrees.
This can be useful when a negative result from atan op cp would require extra work to handle.
Credit for this goes to the 1955 research study ""Approximations for Digital Computers "" by RAND Corporation. This is sheet 11's algorithm which is the fourth-fastest and fourth-least precise. The algorithms on sheets 8-10 are faster but only by a very small degree and are considerably less precise. That study provides an ocb @link #atan op float cp ccb method and that cleanly translates to atan2Deg360 op cp .
